does: This paper develops an information-geometric framework for evaluating the stability and reliability of large language models beyond traditional accuracy metrics. The core contribution is a composite stability score that integrates task utility, entropy (measuring external uncertainty), internal integration, and aligned reflective capacity. The framework is inspired by thermodynamic principles but functions as an interpretable abstraction for how a model's internal structure buffers against perturbations and uncertainty. The authors validate this formulation using the IST-20 benchmarking protocol across 80 model-scenario observations from four contemporary LLMs, demonstrating that their full stability metric consistently outperforms a simpler utility-entropy baseline by a mean improvement of 0.0299 (95% CI: 0.0247–0.0351), suggesting the structural proxies capture meaningful aspects of robustness that aggregate accuracy alone cannot capture.
connects: This work builds on longstanding efforts to move beyond single-metric evaluation of neural networks, extending recent research on LLM reliability, robustness, and interpretability under distribution shift and adversarial perturbations. It responds to the limitations of accuracy-centric benchmarking by incorporating ideas from information theory and thermodynamics, connecting to information-geometric approaches in machine learning that treat probability distributions geometrically. The paper extends work on internal model properties—attention mechanisms, representational alignment, and structural coherence—as predictors of model behavior under stress. It also engages with emerging frameworks for characterizing LLM stability through uncertainty quantification and entropy-based measures, positioning itself within broader initiatives to develop multi-dimensional evaluation protocols for trustworthy AI deployment.
enables: This framework opens pathways for more rigorous stability certification of LLMs in high-stakes deployment scenarios where single-point accuracy estimates are insufficient for assessing reliability. The composite stability metric and its components could inform model selection, fine-tuning strategies, and safety protocols by identifying which internal structural properties most effectively mediate model robustness. The formulation may enable predictive approaches to anticipate failure modes under distribution shift or adversarial conditions without extensive additional testing. Future work could extend these measurements across broader domains and perturbation types, develop closed-form relationships between internal integration and stability, and explore whether the framework's proxies are causal mechanisms or merely correlates, potentially leading to targeted interventions that strengthen model resilience by design.
